URL: https://www.britannica.com/topic/vertical-farming

SUMMARY:
Vertical farming Vertical farming # vertical farming vertical farming, agricultural technique in which crops are grown indoors in vertical, stacked layers with no soil. As a form of controlled environment agriculture (CEA), vertical farming relies wholly on human-made conditions and inputs to provide optimal growing conditions for crops. Another downside to vertical farming is that only a subset of crops can be grown in such settings and not all of those are considered profitable. There is also a debate concerning whether crops grown in a vertical farm can be considered organic. Although the issue remains the subject of legal battles, in 2017 the U.S. Department of Agriculture exempted hydroponic and aquaponic crops from the soil mandate, allowing them to be certified as organic; this includes crops grown in vertical hydroponic and aquaponic systems.

# vertical farming

Our editors will review what you’ve submitted and determine whether to revise the article.

**vertical farming**, agricultural technique in which crops are grown indoors in vertical, stacked layers with no [soil](https://www.britannica.com/science/soil). Modern vertical farming emerged in the late 20th century and has been touted as a means to produce [food](https://www.britannica.com/topic/food) in urban areas and other locations that are not [conducive](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/conducive) to traditional farming. As a form of controlled [environment](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/environment) agriculture (CEA), vertical farming relies wholly on human-made conditions and inputs to provide optimal growing conditions for crops.

## History

![The growing trend of vertical farming](https://cdn.britannica.com/91/240891-138-16E966DC/Vertical-Farming.jpg?w=800&h=450&c=crop)

The modern concept of vertical farming was introduced by Dickson Despommier, a professor of [environmental science](https://www.britannica.com/science/environmental-science). In 1999 Despommier and a group of his graduate students at [Columbia University](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Columbia-University) conceived of a multistory building that could yield more crops with less acreage than traditional [farming](https://www.britannica.com/topic/agriculture) methods. Despommier later [described his vision for vertical farming](https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/the-rise-of-vertical-farms/#:~:text=Plants%20grown%20in%20high%2Drise,transport%20from%20distant%20rural%20farms.), stating that “plants grown in high-rise buildings erected on now vacant city lots and in large, multistory rooftop greenhouses could produce food year-round using significantly less water, producing little waste, with less risk of [infectious](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/infectious) diseases, and no need for fossil-fueled machinery or transport from distant rural farms.”

Variations of vertical farming existed before Despommier conceptualized the modern version: Babylonians practiced an early form of vertical farming by creating [hanging gardens](https://www.britannica.com/place/Hanging-Gardens-of-Babylon), and Europeans in the 1600s grew produce on “fruit walls” that absorbed heat during the day and released it at night.

## Systems and crops

![Innovatus farm](https://cdn.britannica.com/48/259048-050-1AF69655/worker-inspects-lettuce-plants-at-high-tech-innovatus-farm-in-fuji-city-japan.jpg?w=300)

Modern vertical farms can be housed in a wide variety of buildings, including skyscrapers, warehouses, and shipping containers, and smaller setups are also used in farm-to-table restaurants. There are three main soilless methods used in vertical farming operations: [hydroponics](https://www.britannica.com/topic/hydroponics), [aquaponics](https://www.britannica.com/technology/aquaponics), and aeroponics. [Hydroponic systems](https://www.britannica.com/topic/hydroponics) grow plants by submerging their roots in nutrient solution, with or without the mechanical support of an [inert](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/inert) medium such as [sand](https://www.britannica.com/science/sand), [gravel](https://www.britannica.com/science/gravel), or [perlite](https://www.britannica.com/science/perlite). [Aquaponic systems](https://www.britannica.com/technology/aquaponics) expand upon hydroponic systems by incorporating fish and [nitrifying bacteria](https://www.britannica.com/science/nitrifying-bacterium) in a closed loop system; the bacteria convert fish waste into a nutrient source for the plants. [Aeroponic systems](https://www.britannica.com/science/aeroponics), developed by [NASA](https://www.britannica.com/topic/NASA) in the 1990s, grow plants in an air or mist environment.

Given the limited space available in vertical farming systems, most plants are fairly small in size. Popular and cost-effective options include leafy greens, microgreens, [herbs](https://www.britannica.com/topic/spice-food), and [strawberries](https://www.britannica.com/plant/strawberry), which grow relatively quickly and thus take less energy to grow than other crops. Leaf crops are particularly [straightforward](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/straightforward) for vertical farming because they can be harvested whole—allowing the next crop to be planted—and do not require [pollination](https://www.britannica.com/science/pollination) like most [fruit](https://www.britannica.com/science/fruit-plant-reproductive-body), [vegetable](https://www.britannica.com/topic/vegetable), or seed crops. Vegetable crops such as [cucumbers](https://www.britannica.com/plant/cucumber) and [tomatoes](https://www.britannica.com/plant/tomato) are sometimes grown but are not widespread. Traditional row crops, such as [corn](https://www.britannica.com/plant/corn-plant) and [soybeans](https://www.britannica.com/plant/soybean), and [cereal grains](https://www.britannica.com/topic/cereal), such as [rice](https://www.britannica.com/plant/rice) and [wheat](https://www.britannica.com/plant/wheat), are generally not suitable candidates for vertical farming operations.

## Advantages and disadvantages

Proponents of vertical farming cite many benefits over traditional, open-field farming. Perhaps the largest benefit of vertical farming is the ability to produce a high yield of crops in a relatively small area year-round. Vertical farming can permit crop production in areas with suboptimal conditions for traditional farming, such as deserts or urban locales. Being indoors, crops are not subject to extreme weather occurrences or seasonal conditions that can limit production, and they require fewer or no [pesticides](https://www.britannica.com/technology/pesticide). There are also environmental benefits to vertical farming, including limited water usage and reduced emissions from farming equipment like tractors. Finally, vertical farming may be safer for farm workers who are not exposed to traditional agricultural occupational hazards, such as equipment injuries and chemical exposure. It has also reduced transportation costs.

However, critics point to disadvantages that may outweigh the benefits of vertical farming. In urban settings, finding locations for vertical farming can be expensive, and start-up costs are often very high. In addition, there is a steep learning curve to the systems involved, and small errors can affect the whole crop. The systems are also [vulnerable](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/vulnerable) to equipment failure or power outage, which can kill the plants within a few hours. While vertical farms may conserve water and have fewer [carbon dioxide](https://www.britannica.com/science/carbon-dioxide) emissions from farming equipment, the systems require [electricity](https://www.britannica.com/science/electricity) for artificial light, pumps, and [HVAC systems](https://www.britannica.com/technology/HVAC) and can generate a larger [carbon footprint](https://www.britannica.com/science/carbon-footprint) than traditional farms, depending on the source of the electricity. Another downside to vertical farming is that only a subset of crops can be grown in such settings and not all of those are considered profitable. Crops that require pollination are at a particular disadvantage as [pollinators](https://www.britannica.com/science/major-types-of-pollinators) are not generally present indoors; such crops need to be hand-pollinated, a costly and labor-intensive process.

![](https://cdn.britannica.com/marketing/BlueThistle.webp)

There is also a debate concerning whether crops grown in a vertical farm can be considered [organic](https://www.britannica.com/topic/organic-food). In the [United States](https://www.britannica.com/place/United-States), the 1990 Organic Foods Production Act stipulates that to be certified organic, a plan for a farming operation “shall contain provisions designed to foster soil fertility, primarily through the management of the organic content of the soil through proper tillage, [crop rotation](https://www.britannica.com/topic/crop-rotation), and manuring.” Given that vertical farms do not use soil, some have argued that they should be ineligible for organic certification. Many vertical farming operations [abide](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/abide) by [organic farming](https://www.britannica.com/topic/organic-farming) standards, including the application of organic pesticides when necessary and the use of non-synthetic fertilizers. Although the issue remains the subject of legal battles, in 2017 the [U.S. Department of Agriculture](https://www.britannica.com/topic/US-Department-of-Agriculture) exempted hydroponic and aquaponic crops from the soil [mandate](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/mandate), allowing them to be certified as organic; this includes crops grown in vertical hydroponic and aquaponic systems. Aeroponic systems were notably rejected. Similarly, vertical farms in Singapore can apply for organic certification. However, [European Union](https://www.britannica.com/topic/European-Union) standards exclude vertically farmed plants from being labeled as organic, specifying that “organic production is only possible when plants are grown naturally in soil.”

SUMMARY:
(July 2023)(Learn how and when to remove this message) Vertical farming is the practice of growing crops in vertically and horizontally stacked layers. [1] It often incorporates controlled-environment agriculture, which aims to optimize plant growth, and soilless farming techniques such as hydroponics, aquaponics, and aeroponics. [1] Some common choices of structures to house vertical farming systems include buildings, shipping containers, underground tunnels, and abandoned mine shafts. [11] The main advantage of utilizing vertical farming technologies is the increased crop yield that comes with a smaller unit area of land requirement. Additionally, crops are resistant to weather disruptions because of their placement indoors, meaning fewer crops lost to extreme or unexpected weather occurrences.

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://en.wikipedia.org/static/images/donate/donate.gif)
![Wikipedia](/static/images/mobile/copyright/wikipedia-wordmark-en.svg)

# Vertical farming

|  |
| --- |
| The examples and perspective in this article **may not represent a [worldwide view](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Countering_systemic_bias "Wikipedia:WikiProject Countering systemic bias") of the subject**. You may [improve this article](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Vertical_farming&action=edit), discuss the issue on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Vertical_farming "Talk:Vertical farming"), or [create a new article](/wiki/Wikipedia:Article_wizard "Wikipedia:Article wizard"), as appropriate. *(July 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

**Vertical farming** is the practice of growing crops in vertically and horizontally stacked layers.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) It often incorporates [controlled-environment agriculture](/wiki/Controlled-environment_agriculture "Controlled-environment agriculture"), which aims to optimize plant growth, and soilless farming techniques such as [hydroponics](/wiki/Hydroponics "Hydroponics"), [aquaponics](/wiki/Aquaponics "Aquaponics"), and [aeroponics](/wiki/Aeroponics "Aeroponics").[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1) Some common choices of structures to house vertical farming systems include buildings, shipping containers, underground tunnels, and abandoned mine shafts.

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/VertiCrop.jpg/250px-VertiCrop.jpg)

The modern concept of vertical farming was proposed in 1999 by [Dickson Despommier](/wiki/Dickson_Despommier "Dickson Despommier"), professor of Public and Environmental Health at Columbia University.[[2]](#cite_note-:8-2) Despommier and his students came up with a design of a skyscraper farm that could feed 50,000 people.[[3]](#cite_note-:6-3) Although the design has not yet been built, it successfully popularized the idea of vertical farming.[[3]](#cite_note-:6-3) Current applications of vertical farming coupled with other state-of-the-art technologies, such as specialized [LED](/wiki/Light-emitting_diode "Light-emitting diode") lights, have resulted in over 10 times the crop yield as would be received through traditional farming methods.[[4]](#cite_note-:10-4) There have been several different means of implementing vertical farming systems into communities such as: [Paignton](/wiki/Paignton "Paignton"),[[5]](#cite_note-:14-5) [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"),[[6]](#cite_note-:15-6) [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"),[[7]](#cite_note-:16-7) [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"),[[8]](#cite_note-:17-8) [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"),[[9]](#cite_note-:18-9) [London](/wiki/London "London"),[[10]](#cite_note-:19-10) [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"),[[4]](#cite_note-:10-4) and [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire").[[11]](#cite_note-:20-11)

The main advantage of utilizing vertical farming technologies is the increased crop yield that comes with a smaller unit area of land requirement.[[12]](#cite_note-:9-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) The increased ability to cultivate a larger variety of crops at once because crops do not share the same plots of land while growing is another sought-after advantage. Additionally, crops are resistant to weather disruptions because of their placement indoors, meaning fewer crops lost to extreme or unexpected weather occurrences. Lastly, because of its limited land usage, vertical farming is less disruptive to the native plants and animals, leading to further conservation of the local flora and fauna.[[14]](#cite_note-:12-14)

Vertical farming technologies face economic challenges with large start-up costs compared to traditional farms. They cannot grow all types of crops but can be cost-effective for high value products such as salad vegetables.[[15]](#cite_note-HarveyBBC_2024-15) Vertical farms also face large energy demands due to the use of supplementary light like LEDs. The buildings also need excellent control of temperature, humidity and water supplies. Moreover, if [non-renewable energy](/wiki/Non-renewable_energy "Non-renewable energy") is used to meet these energy demands, vertical farms could produce more pollution than traditional farms or [greenhouses](/wiki/Greenhouse "Greenhouse").

## Contents

## Types

The term "vertical farming" was coined by Gilbert Ellis Bailey in 1915 in his book *Vertical Farming*. His use of the term differs from the current meaning—he wrote about [farming](/wiki/Farming "Farming") with a special interest in soil origin, its nutrient content and the view of plant life as "vertical" life forms, specifically relating to their underground root structures.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Modern usage of the term "vertical farming" usually refers to growing plants in layers, whether in a multistorey skyscraper, used warehouse, or shipping container.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Mixed-use skyscrapers

Mixed-use skyscrapers were proposed and built by architect [Ken Yeang](/wiki/Ken_Yeang "Ken Yeang"). Yeang proposes that instead of hermetically sealed mass-produced agriculture, plant life should be cultivated within open air, mixed-use skyscrapers for climate control and consumption. This version of vertical farming is based upon personal or community use rather than the wholesale production and distribution that aspires to feed an entire city.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Despommier's skyscrapers

Ecologist [Dickson Despommier](/wiki/Dickson_Despommier "Dickson Despommier") argues that vertical farming is legitimate for environmental reasons. He claims that the cultivation of plant life within skyscrapers will require less [embodied energy](/wiki/Embodied_energy "Embodied energy") and produce less pollution than some methods of producing plant life on natural landscapes. By shifting to vertical farms, Despommier believes that farmland will return to its natural state (i.e., forests), which would help reverse the effects of climate change. He moreover claims that natural landscapes are too toxic for natural agricultural production. Vertical farming would remove some of the parasitic risks associated with farming.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Despommier's concept of the vertical farm emerged in 1999 at [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"). It promotes the mass cultivation of plant life for commercial purposes in skyscrapers.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Stackable shipping containers

Several companies have developed stacking recycled [shipping containers](/wiki/Shipping_container "Shipping container") in urban settings. The shipping containers serve as standardized, modular environmental chambers for growing. By stacking the shipping containers, higher density in terms of produce yield/square foot is possible. But, the stacked containers pose the challenge of how to effectively and affordably access the stacked levels. Brighterside Consulting created a complete off-grid container system.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Freight Farms](/wiki/Freight_Farms "Freight Farms") produces the "Greenery" that is a complete [farm-to-table](/wiki/Farm-to-table "Farm-to-table") system outfitted with vertical hydroponics, [LED](/wiki/LED "LED") lighting and intuitive climate controls built within a 12 m × 2.4 m shipping container.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Podponics built a vertical farm in Atlanta consisting of over 100 stacked "growpods", but reportedly went bankrupt in May 2016.[[21]](#cite_note-21) A similar farm is under construction in Oman.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

TerraFarms offer a system of 40-foot shipping containers, which include computer vision integrated with an [artificial neural network](/wiki/Artificial_neural_network "Artificial neural network") to monitor the plants;[[22]](#cite_note-localrootsfarms-how-we-farm-tech-22) and are remotely monitored from California. It is claimed that the TerraFarm system "has achieved cost parity with traditional, outdoor farming"[[23]](#cite_note-arstechnica-science-2017-12-local-roots-farm-in-a-box-23) with each unit producing the equivalent of "three to five acres [one to two hectares] of farmland", using 97% less water[[24]](#cite_note-https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2017/jul/18/cli-cha-los-ang-24) through water recapture and harvesting the evaporated water through the air conditioning.[[25]](#cite_note-25) As of December 2017[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Vertical_farming&action=edit) the TerraFarm system was in commercial operation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### In abandoned mine shafts

Vertical farming in abandoned mine shafts is termed "deep farming", and is proposed to take advantage of consistent underground temperatures and locations near or in urban areas. It would also be able to use nearby groundwater, thereby reducing the cost of providing water to the farm.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Technology

Lighting can be natural or via LEDs. As of 2018 commercial LEDs were about 28% efficient,[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] which keeps the cost of produce high and prevents vertical farms from competing in regions where cheap vegetables are abundant.[[27]](#cite_note-:0-27) Energy costs can be reduced because full-spectrum white light is not required. Instead, red and blue or purple light can be generated with less electricity.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## History

One of the earliest drawings of a tall building that cultivates food was published in *[Life Magazine](/wiki/Life_Magazine "Life Magazine")* in 2009.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The reproduced drawings feature vertically stacked homesteads set amidst a farming landscape. This proposal can be seen in [Rem Koolhaas](/wiki/Rem_Koolhaas "Rem Koolhaas")'s *Delirious New York*. Koolhaas wrote that this theorem is 'The Skyscraper as Utopian device for the production of unlimited numbers of virgin sites on a metropolitan location'.[[29]](#cite_note-Koolhaas2014-29)

### Hydroponicum

Early architectural proposals that contribute to VF include [Le Corbusier](/wiki/Le_Corbusier "Le Corbusier")'s Immeubles-Villas (1922) and SITE's Highrise of Homes (1972).[[30]](#cite_note-abc-30) SITE's Highrise of Homes is a near revival of the 1909 *Life Magazine* Theorem.[[31]](#cite_note-Eaton2002-31) Built examples of tower hydroponicums are documented in *The Glass House* by John Hix. Images of the vertical farms at the School of Gardeners in Langenlois, Austria, and the glass tower at the Vienna International Horticulture Exhibition (1964) show that vertical farms existed.[[32]](#cite_note-Hix,_John_1974-32) The technological precedents that make vertical farming possible can be traced back to horticultural history through the development of [greenhouse](/wiki/Greenhouse "Greenhouse") and [hydroponic](/wiki/Hydroponics "Hydroponics") technology. Early [hydroponicums](/wiki/Hydroponicum "Hydroponicum") integrated hydroponic technology into building systems. These [horticultural building systems](/wiki/Horticultural_building_system "Horticultural building system") evolved from greenhouse technology. The British Interplanetary Society developed a [hydroponicum](/wiki/Hydroponicum "Hydroponicum") for lunar conditions, while other building prototypes were developed during the early days of space exploration. The first Tower Hydroponic Units were developed in Armenia.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The Armenian tower hydroponicums are the first built examples of a vertical farm, and are documented in Sholto Douglas' *[Hydroponics](/wiki/Hydroponics "Hydroponics"): The Bengal System,* first published in 1951 with data from the then-[East Pakistan](/wiki/East_Pakistan "East Pakistan"), today's [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), and the [Indian](/wiki/India "India") state of [West Bengal](/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal").[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-Sholto_Douglas_1975-36)

Later precursors that have been published, or built, are Ken Yeang's Bioclimatic Skyscraper (Menara Mesiniaga, built 1992); MVRDV's PigCity, 2000; MVRDV's Meta City/ Datatown (1998–2000); Pich-Aguilera's Garden Towers (2001).[[30]](#cite_note-abc-30)

Ken Yeang is perhaps the most widely known architect who has promoted the idea of the 'mixed-use' Bioclimatic Skyscraper which combines living units and food production.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Vertical farm

[Dickson Despommier](/wiki/Dickson_Despommier "Dickson Despommier") is a professor of [environmental health](/wiki/Environmental_health "Environmental health") sciences and [microbiology](/wiki/Microbiology "Microbiology"). He reopened the topic of VF in 1999 with graduate students in a medical ecology class. He speculated that a 30-floor farm on one city block could provide food for 50,000 people including vegetables, fruit, eggs and meat, explaining that hydroponic crops could be grown on upper floors; while the lower floors would be suited for chickens and fish that eat plant waste.

Although many of Despommier's suggestions have been challenged from an environmental science and engineering point of view, Despommier successfully popularized his assertion that food production can be transformed. Critics claimed that the additional energy needed for artificial lighting, heating and other operations would outweigh the benefit of the building's close proximity to the areas of consumption.[[37]](#cite_note-Nelson-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Despommier originally challenged his class to feed the entire population of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") (about 2,000,000 people) using only 5 hectares (13 acres) of [rooftop gardens](/wiki/Rooftop_gardens "Rooftop gardens"). The class calculated that rooftop gardening methods could feed only 2% of the population. Unsatisfied with the results, Despommier made an off-the-cuff suggestion of growing plants indoors, vertically. By 2001 the first outline of a vertical farm was introduced. In an interview Despommier described how vertical farms would function:

Each floor will have its own watering and nutrient monitoring systems. There will be sensors for every single plant that tracks how much and what kinds of nutrients the plant has absorbed. You'll even have systems to monitor plant diseases by employing DNA chip technologies that detect the presence of plant pathogens by simply sampling the air and using snippets from various viral and bacterial infections. It's very easy to do.
Moreover, a gas [chromatograph](/wiki/Chromatograph "Chromatograph") will tell us when to pick the plant by analyzing which [flavenoids](/wiki/Flavonoid "Flavonoid") the produce contains. These flavonoids are what gives the food the flavors you're so fond of, particularly for more aromatic produce like tomatoes and peppers. These are all right-off-the-shelf technologies. The ability to construct a vertical farm exists now. We don't have to make anything new.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Architectural designs were independently produced by designers Chris Jacobs, Andrew Kranis and Gordon Graff.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Mass media attention began with an article written in *[New York](/wiki/New_York_(magazine) "New York (magazine)")* magazine,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] followed by others,[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-sciam-45) as well as radio and television features.

In 2011, the Plant in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") was building an [anaerobic digester](/wiki/Anaerobic_digester "Anaerobic digester") into the building. This will allow the farm to operate off the energy grid. Moreover, the anaerobic digester will be recycling waste from nearby businesses that would otherwise go into landfills.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

In 2013, the [Association for Vertical Farming](/wiki/Association_for_Vertical_Farming "Association for Vertical Farming") was founded in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), Germany.

As of 2014, Vertical Fresh Farms was operating in [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), New York, specializing in [salad greens](/wiki/Salad_greens "Salad greens"), [herbs](/wiki/Herb "Herb") and sprouts.[[47]](#cite_note-47) In March the world's then largest vertical farm opened in Scranton, Pennsylvania, built by Green Spirit Farms (GSF). The firm is housed in a single-storey building covering 3.25 hectares, with racks stacked six high to house 17 million plants. The farm was to grow 14 lettuce crops per year, as well as spinach, kale, tomatoes, peppers, basil and strawberries. Water is scavenged from the farm's atmosphere with a [dehumidifier](/wiki/Dehumidifier "Dehumidifier").[[27]](#cite_note-:0-27)

Kyoto-based Nuvege (pronounced "new veggie") operates a windowless farm. Its LED lighting is tuned to service two types of chlorophyll, one preferring red light and the other blue. Nuvege produces 6 million lettuce heads a year.[[27]](#cite_note-:0-27)

The US Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency ([DARPA](/wiki/DARPA "DARPA")) operates an 18-storey project that produces [genetically modified plants](/wiki/Genetically_modified_plant "Genetically modified plant") that make [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein") useful in [vaccines](/wiki/Vaccine "Vaccine").[[27]](#cite_note-:0-27)

Plenty has designed a new AI-controlled modular grow system for multiple crops; they are opening a farm in [Chesterfield](/wiki/Chesterfield_County,_Virginia "Chesterfield County, Virginia"), Virginia that will grow more than 1.8 million kilograms (4 million pounds) of strawberries each year. The farm uses 97% less land and 97% less water than traditional farming.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

## Advantages

Many of VF's potential benefits are obtained from scaling up hydroponic or [aeroponic](/wiki/Aeroponic "Aeroponic") growing methods.[[49]](#cite_note-verticalfarm1-49)

A 2018 study estimated that the value of four ecosystem services provided by existing vegetation in urban areas was on the order of $33 billion annually. The study's quantitative framework projected annual food production of 100–180 million tonnes, energy savings ranging from 14 to 15 billion kilowatt-hours, nitrogen sequestration between 100,000 and 170,000 tonnes and stormwater runoff reductions between 45 and 57 billion cubic metres annually. Food production, nitrogen fixation, energy savings, pollination, climate regulation, soil formation and biological pest control could be worth as much as $80–160 billion annually.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### Reduced need for farmland

It is estimated that by the year 2050, the world's population will increase by 3 billion people and close to 80% will live in urban areas.[[51]](#cite_note-vf-51) Vertical farms have the potential to reduce or eliminate the need to create additional farmland.[[51]](#cite_note-vf-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Increased crop production

Unlike traditional farming in non-tropical areas, indoor farming can produce crops year-round. All-season farming multiplies the productivity of the farmed surface by a factor of 4 to 6 depending on the crop. With crops such as strawberries, the factor may be as high as 30.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)

Furthermore, as the crops would be consumed where they are grown, long-distance transport with its accompanying time delays, should reduce spoilage, infestation and energy needs. Globally some 30% of harvested crops are wasted due to spoilage and infestation, though this number is much lower in developed nations.[[45]](#cite_note-sciam-45)

Despommier suggests that once dwarf versions of crops (e.g., [dwarf wheat](/wiki/Dwarf_wheat "Dwarf wheat") which is smaller in size but richer in nutrients[[56]](#cite_note-nasa-56)), year-round crops and "stacker" plant holders are accounted for, a 30-storey building with a base of a building block (2 hectares or 5 acres) would yield a yearly crop analogous to that of 1,000 hectares (2,400 acres) of traditional farming.[[45]](#cite_note-sciam-45)

### Weather disruption

Crops grown in traditional outdoor farming depend on supportive weather, and suffer from undesirable temperatures rain, monsoon, hailstorm, tornado, flooding, wildfires and drought.[[49]](#cite_note-verticalfarm1-49) "Three recent floods (in 1993, 2007 and 2008) cost the United States billions of dollars in lost crops, with even more devastating losses in topsoil. Changes in rain patterns and temperature could diminish India's agricultural output by 30% by the end of the century."[[57]](#cite_note-57)

VF productivity is mostly independent of weather, although earthquakes and tornadoes still pose threats.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The issue of adverse weather conditions is especially relevant for arctic and sub-arctic areas like [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") and northern [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") where traditional farming is largely impossible. Food insecurity has been a long-standing problem in remote northern communities where fresh produce has to be shipped large distances resulting in high costs and poor nutrition.[[58]](#cite_note-58) Container-based farms can provide fresh produce year-round at a lower cost than shipping in supplies from more southerly locations with a number of farms operating in locations such as [Churchill, Manitoba](/wiki/Churchill,_Manitoba "Churchill, Manitoba") and [Unalaska, Alaska](/wiki/Unalaska,_Alaska "Unalaska, Alaska")[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) As with disruption to crop growing, local container-based farms are also less susceptible to disruption than the long supply chains necessary to deliver traditionally grown produce to remote communities. Food prices in Churchill spiked substantially after floods in May and June 2017 forced the closure of the rail line that forms the only permanent overland connection between Churchill and the rest of Canada.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

### Conservation

Up to 20 units of outdoor farmland per unit of VF could return to its natural state,[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) due to VF's increased productivity.

Vertical farming would thus reduce the amount of farmland, thus saving many natural resources.[[45]](#cite_note-sciam-45) Deforestation and [desertification](/wiki/Desertification "Desertification") caused by agricultural encroachment on natural [biomes](/wiki/Biome "Biome") could be avoided. Producing food indoors reduces or eliminates conventional plowing, planting, and harvesting by farm machinery, protecting soil and reducing emissions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Resource scarcity

The scarcity of fertilizer components like phosphorus[[64]](#cite_note-phosphorus-64) poses a threat to industrial agriculture. The closed-cycle design of vertical farm systems minimizes the loss of nutrients, while traditional field agriculture loses nutrients to runoff and leeching.[[65]](#cite_note-runoff-65)

### Mass extinction

Withdrawing human activity from large areas of the Earth's land surface may be necessary to address [anthropogenic mass extinctions](/wiki/Holocene_extinction_event "Holocene extinction event").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Traditional agriculture disrupts wild populations and may be unethical given a viable alternative. One study showed that [wood mouse](/wiki/Wood_mouse "Wood mouse") populations dropped from 25 per hectare to 5 per hectare after harvest, estimating 10 animals killed per hectare each year with conventional farming.[[66]](#cite_note-sldavis-66) In comparison, vertical farming would cause nominal harm to wildlife.[[66]](#cite_note-sldavis-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67)

### Human health

Traditional farming is a hazardous occupation that often affects the health of farmers. Such risks include: exposure to infectious agents such as [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria") and [schistosomes](/wiki/Schistosomes "Schistosomes"), as well as soil-borne microbes, exposure to toxic pesticides and fungicides, confrontations with wildlife such as venomous snakes, and injuries that can occur when using large industrial farming equipment. VF reduces some of these risks.[[49]](#cite_note-verticalfarm1-49) The modern industrial food system makes unhealthy food cheap while fresh produce is more expensive, encouraging poor eating habits. These habits lead to health problems such as [obesity](/wiki/Obesity "Obesity"), [heart disease](/wiki/Heart_disease "Heart disease") and [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Poverty and culture

Food insecurity is one of the primary factors leading to [absolute poverty](/wiki/Absolute_poverty "Absolute poverty"). Constructing farms will allow continued growth of culturally significant food items without sacrificing sustainability or basic needs, which can be significant to the recovery of a society from poverty.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

### Urban growth

Vertical farming, used in conjunction with other technologies and socioeconomic practices, could allow cities to expand while remaining substantially self-sufficient in food. This would allow large urban centers to grow without food constraints.[[49]](#cite_note-verticalfarm1-49)

### Energy sustainability

Vertical farms could exploit [methane digesters](/wiki/Methane_digesters "Methane digesters") to generate energy. Methane digesters could be built on site to transform the organic waste generated at the farm into [biogas](/wiki/Biogas "Biogas") that is generally composed of 65% methane along with other gases. This biogas could then be burned to generate electricity for the greenhouse.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

## Problems

### Economics

Vertical farms require substantial start-up funding and some start-up companies have not been able to achieve a profit before going bankrupt.[[70]](#cite_note-Baraniuk2023-70)
Opponents question the potential profitability of vertical farming.[[71]](#cite_note-71) Its economic and environmental benefits rest partly on the concept of minimizing [food miles](/wiki/Food_miles "Food miles"), the distance that food travels from farm to consumer.[*[original research?](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research "Wikipedia:No original research")*] However, a recent analysis suggests that transportation is only a minor contributor to the economic and environmental costs of supplying food to urban populations. The analysis concluded that "food miles are, at best, a marketing fad".[[72]](#cite_note-72) Thus the facility would have to lower costs or charge higher prices to justify remaining in a city.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Similarly, if power needs are met by fossil fuels, the environmental effect may be a net loss;[[73]](#cite_note-73) even building low-carbon capacity to power the farms may not make as much sense as simply leaving traditional farms in place, while burning less coal.

The initial building costs would exceed $100 million for a 60-hectare vertical farm.[[74]](#cite_note-74) Office [occupancy costs](/wiki/Occupancy_cost "Occupancy cost") can be high in major cities, with office space in cities such as [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"), [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai"), [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai "Dubai"), [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), [Zurich](/wiki/Zurich "Zurich"), and [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo "São Paulo") ranging from $1850 to $880 per square metre.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

The developers of the TerraFarm system produced from second hand, 40 foot shipping containers claimed that their system "has achieved cost parity with traditional, outdoor farming".[[23]](#cite_note-arstechnica-science-2017-12-local-roots-farm-in-a-box-23)

### Energy use

During the growing season, the sun shines on a vertical surface at an extreme angle such that much less light is available to crops than when they are planted on flat land. Therefore, supplemental light would be required. [Bruce Bugbee](/wiki/Bruce_Bugbee "Bruce Bugbee") claimed that the power demands of vertical farming would be uncompetitive with traditional farms using only natural light.[[37]](#cite_note-Nelson-37)[[76]](#cite_note-76) Environmental writer [George Monbiot](/wiki/George_Monbiot "George Monbiot") calculated that the cost of providing enough supplementary light to grow the grain for a single loaf would be about $15.[[77]](#cite_note-77) An article in the Economist argued that "even though crops growing in a glass skyscraper will get some natural sunlight during the day, it won't be enough" and "the cost of powering artificial lights will make indoor farming prohibitively expensive".[[78]](#cite_note-78)

As "The Vertical Farm" proposes a controlled environment, heating and cooling costs will resemble those of any other tower. Plumbing and elevator systems are necessary to distribute nutrients and water. In the northern continental United States, fossil fuel heating cost can be over $200,000 per hectare.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

Jones Food Company in [Gloucestershire](/wiki/Gloucestershire "Gloucestershire"), England opened a farm in 2024 with 14,500 square metres (156,000 sq ft) of growing space, powered only by [renewable electricity](/wiki/Renewable_electricity "Renewable electricity").[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)

### Pollution

Depending on the method of electricity generation

enriched_points.expanded_content: Farming has transformed from ancient techniques of subsistence agriculture to advanced mechanized methods, incorporating irrigation, crop rotation, and sustainable practices to enhance productivity and efficiency.
Modern vertical farming emerged in the late 20th century, conceptualized by Dickson Despommier, focusing on urban food production through stacked layers in controlled environments.
Significant milestones include Despommier's skyscraper design in 1999, integration of hydroponics and aquaponics, and the establishment of various vertical farming facilities worldwide.
